pricing.source: OpenAI API pricing, standard short-context rates, 2026-06-24

hardChecks.label: Citation source ids
hardChecks.expectation: Every cited source id exists in the source packet

hardChecks.label: Cost metadata
hardChecks.expectation: estimatedUsd is present when pricing is configured

hardChecks.label: Token metadata
hardChecks.expectation: Input, cached-input, and output token counts are coherent

hardChecks.label: Provider and model metadata
hardChecks.expectation: Provider, model, and prompt version are present

hardChecks.label: Judge input boundary
hardChecks.expectation: Judge prompt omits eval-only object keys and holdout tuning fields

hardChecks.label: Judge claim coverage
hardChecks.expectation: LLM judge returns exactly one judgment for every briefing claim

claimJudgments.claimText: Do not expand coverage via broad runs until failures are clustered; smaller reviewed batches made repeated citation issues easier to group and yielded more actionable prompt changes.
claimJudgments.explanation: S1 says teams need smaller eval batches with visible failure clusters before expanding model coverage, and that the smaller batch made repeated citation issues easier to group and produced more actionable prompt changes.

claimJudgments.claimText: Reviewer throughput and perceived speed regressed in the last eval review because failures were not grouped by pattern; reviewers preferred fewer cases with clearer evidence over larger runs requiring manual interpretation per row.
missingEvidence: Evidence of actual reviewer throughput regression, as opposed to reviewers feeling slower than expected.
claimJudgments.explanation: S5 supports that the review felt slower because failures were not grouped and that reviewers preferred fewer cases with clearer evidence over larger manually interpreted runs. It does not directly measure or state throughput regression.

claimJudgments.claimText: Coverage improvements can mask regressions in grounding; citation support needs an explicit shipping gate that blocks releases when outputs contain unsupported claims.
claimJudgments.explanation: S2 states coverage can improve while grounding regresses and citation support needs an explicit shipping gate/release block when candidate outputs make unsupported claims, especially in customer-facing recommendations.

claimJudgments.claimText: Long traces are valuable for debugging why a claim failed, but uncontrolled retries drove a 1.34x cost increase; trace retention is acceptable if retries are capped and summarized in the run manifest (retry count, cost ratio, and what the trace resolved).
claimJudgments.explanation: S3 directly supports trace debugging value, the 1.34x cost increase from unchecked retries, and finance acceptance of detailed traces if retries are capped and the manifest includes retry count, cost ratio, and a short explanation of trace utility.

claimJudgments.claimText: Holdout cases should be visible only as summaries/aggregate scores in dashboards; labels/expected answers must stay out of prompt-iteration workflows to reduce memorization risk.
claimJudgments.explanation: S4 says holdout cases should appear in dashboards as summaries and aggregate scores, while labels and expected answers should stay out of prompt-editing workflows to avoid overfitting/memorizing visible examples.

recommendationJudgment.explanation: The recommendation directly addresses whether to expand automated evals and balances coverage, citation support, trace utility, and cost. It is well supported by S1/S5 on clustering and smaller visible batches, S2 on grounding gates, S3 on trace utility and retry cost controls, S4 on holdout handling, and S6 on one more visible batch before live orchestration with expansion only if grounding improves without exceeding the cost guardrail. The main minor issue is that the briefing did not cite S6 despite relying on a recommendation that S6 states directly.

citationSupport.note: S1 appears in fully supported claim-level judgments.
S5 appears in a partially supported or unsupported claim judgment.
S2 appears in fully supported claim-level judgments.
S3 appears in fully supported claim-level judgments.
S4 appears in fully supported claim-level judgments.

notes: Hybrid evaluator output: deterministic hard checks plus structured LLM judge evidence. Manual spot checks are still required before claiming product improvement.
